coredata.dc:title: Preparation of photoluminescent nanocomposite by one-step method from cobalt oxide-Jeffamine based on novel polymer dot as a high-performance and ultra-stable symmetric supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Alimola, Fariba
Arsalani, Nasser
Ahadzadeh, Iraj
coredata.dc:description: Polymer dots are a new class of quantum dots that have attracted much attention from researchers. Based on, in this work, for the first time, using a one-step method, the new PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite with a quantum yield of 65.45%, based on the polymer dot of jeffamine was synthesized, which has ideal supercapacitor properties. The new polymer dot-based Jeffamine (PDJA) was first synthesized using a Jeffamine polymer and simultaneously composited with cobalt oxide by the solvothermal method. The photoluminescence properties and supercapacitor performance of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA were investigated. The PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA were prepared as electrode materials, and analysis of CV, GCD, and EIS was performed that the specific capacitance of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material and the PDJA electrode material are 978 F.g−1 and 283 F.g−1 at 50 mV/s, respectively. Also, the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA have cyclic stability of 98.37% and 91.57% up to 10,000 cycles of cyclic voltammetry at 100 mV/s, respectively. Then, two symmetric supercapacitors were manufactured of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA. Finally, due to the high conductivity of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite active material, the symmetric supercapacitor of PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite turned on the LED lamp.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
Polymer dot
Supercapacitor
Photoluminescence
Jeffamine

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) The TGA curve of PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. b) The FT-IR spectra of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA.

a and b) The FE-SEM analyses of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. c) The elemental mapping of Co, O, N, and C. d) The EDS analysis of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. e) The particle size distribution of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite.

a and b) The TEM analysis of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. c) The FFT image of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. d) The HRTEM analysis of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. e) The SAED analysis of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite.
X-ray diffraction of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA, X-ray diffraction of graphene oxide, and graphite.
a) UV-Vis spectrum of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. b) The PL spectrum of the PDJA and the PL spectrum of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite.
a) The DLS analysis of the PDJA. b) The DLS analysis of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. c) the zeta potential of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA.

a) The CV curve of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite, and the PDJA in the potential window − 0.4–0.8 V at 50 mV/s. b) The CV curve of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite at scan rates of 10–100 mV/s in the potential window of − 0.4–0.8 V. c) The CV curve of the PDJA at scan rates of 10–100 mV/s in the potential window of − 0.4–0.8 V. d) The cyclic stability of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA.

a) The CV curve of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite at 25 mV/s and selected anodic peak and cathodic peaks to check the charge storage mechanism b) Peak current density versus scan rate of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite c) GCD curve of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite from 1 to 5 A g−1 current densities at − 0.4–0.8 V potential window. d) GCD curve of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite from 1 to 5 A g−1 current densities at − 0.4–0.8 V potential window.

The CV curve of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite device, and the PDJA device in the potential window 0–1.5 V at 50 mV/s b) The CV curve of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite at scan rates of 10–100 mV/s in the potential window of 0–1.5 V. c) The CV curve of the PDJA at scan rates of 10–100 mV/s in the potential window of 0–1.5 V. d) The cyclic stability of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite device.

a) GCD curve of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite device from 1–5 A g−1 current densities at 0–1.5 V potential window. b) GCD curve of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite device from 1–5 A g−1 current densities at 0–1.5 V potential window. c) The Nyquist plots of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite device (inset show an equivalent circuit of nanocomposite the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite). d) The grown-up view of the semicircle in the high-frequency part of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite.
The complete steps of PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite synthesis.
Schematic of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite symmetric device and the LED lamp illuminated by the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite symmetric device.
Comparison of stability of different electrode materials in articles.
Fitted parameter of EIS data of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Preparation of photoluminescent nanocomposite by one-step method from cobalt oxide-Jeffamine based on novel polymer dot as a high-performance and ultra-stable symmetric supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Polymer, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Polymer, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Polymer, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Electrochemical Instrumentation and Energy Systems, Department of Physical Chemistry Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Electrochemical Instrumentation and Energy Systems, Department of Physical Chemistry Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Electrochemical Instrumentation and Energy Systems, Department of Physical Chemistry Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymer dots are a new class of quantum dots that have attracted much attention from researchers. Based on, in this work, for the first time, using a one-step method, the new PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite with a quantum yield of 65.45%, based on the polymer dot of jeffamine was synthesized, which has ideal supercapacitor properties. The new polymer dot-based Jeffamine (PDJA) was first synthesized using a Jeffamine polymer and simultaneously composited with cobalt oxide by the solvothermal method. The photoluminescence properties and supercapacitor performance of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA were investigated. The PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA were prepared as electrode materials, and analysis of CV, GCD, and EIS was performed that the specific capacitance of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material and the PDJA electrode material are 978 F.g−1 and 283 F.g−1 at 50 mV/s, respectively. Also, the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA have cyclic stability of 98.37% and 91.57% up to 10,000 cycles of cyclic voltammetry at 100 mV/s, respectively. Then, two symmetric supercapacitors were manufactured of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA. Finally, due to the high conductivity of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite active material, the symmetric supercapacitor of PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite turned on the LED lamp.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Recently, with the increased advancement of science and technology, new energy storage devices such as ion batteries [1], solar cells, and supercapacitors were built to overcome the limitations of traditional batteries [2–4]. Supercapacitors received more attention due to excellent advantages such as high-power density, longer life cycles, and cost-effectiveness, also supercapacitors are the perfect alternative to fossil fuels, which results in reduced environmental problems [5,6]. Despite the stated advantages, supercapacitors have a low energy density. According to Eq. (1), the energy density of supercapacitors can be improved by the increased capacitance (C) or the voltage window (V) [7,8].
(1) E = 1/2 CV2

Because of the above, many efforts have manufactured supercapacitors with, which finally leads to the practical application of supercapacitors in various industries, including electric vehicles, fuel cells, and electronic mobile phones [9]. In recent years, metal oxides have been used in the manufacture of supercapacitors due to their favorable electrochemical properties. Despite the favorable electrochemical properties of supercapacitors, metal oxides have disadvantages such as low electrical conductivity uncontrolled volumetric expansion, and slow ion diffusion in the solid phase, which limits the use of metal oxides in supercapacitors [10–12]. To improve the electrochemical properties of metal oxides, used composites of metal oxides with other materials, including conductive polymers, carbon materials [13,14], and, more recently, quantum dots [15]. Quantum dots have weak electrochemical properties, but the electrochemical properties of quantum dots were improved when combined with metal oxides due to the synergistic effect between the metal oxide and the quantum dots. For example, Yong-Gang et al. synthesized Carbon Dots / NiCo2O4 nanocomposites with different morphologies and investigated their performance in supercapacitors. The results showed the optimal composite has a high energy density of 62 Wh kg−1 [16]. Quantum dots were one of the widely used materials in supercapacitors and were divided into three categories: carbon quantum dots [17], graphene quantum dots, and polymer dots [18]. Polymer dots are a new group of quantum dots that simultaneously have the properties of polymers and quantum dots [19]. Polymer dots have excellent properties such as easy surface modification, processability, and luminescence properties. At all, the luminescence properties of polymer dots depend on their size [20]. Polymer dots have a particle size of 1–50 nm, while polymer dots with a particle size of 1–10 nm have excellent luminescence properties [21]. Due to the mentioned properties, the polymer dots were used for drug delivery, bioimaging [22], biosensors, solar cells, sensor [23], and supercapacitors [24]. In general, for the synthesis of polymer dots were used, the method of nanoprecipitation, miniemulsion, and solvothermal. Non-conjugated polymers and conjugated polymers were used to synthesize polymer dots. he conjugated polymers were used for the synthesis of polymer dots such as polypyrrole [25], polythiophene, polyethylene glycol, and polyaniline that more favorable properties [26,27]. Polymer dots have -OH, -COOH, -NH functional groups on their surface that facilitate the penetration of the electrolyte and improve the electrochemical properties. In this work, for the first time, using a one-step and easy method, the new nanocomposite PDJA-Co3O4 was synthesized with a quantum yield of 65.45% based on the new polymer dots of Jeff amine. The basis synthesis of new polymer dots is Jeffamine polymer, which consists of a set of polyethers and amine groups on both sides of the polyether chain [28]. Jeffamine polymer was an ideal candidate for supercapacitors. The PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite was synthesized by solvothermal method and using Jeffamine polymer and cobalt acetate in the presence of ethanol and dimethylformamide solvents. Finally, the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite was identified by relevant analyses. Also, the luminescence properties of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite investigating by photoluminescence (PL) analysis, and the results of this investigation confirmed the ideal luminescence properties of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. The cyclic voltammetry, charge-discharge, and impedance analyses were performed to check the electrochemical properties of the PDJA-Co3O4 and the PDJA. Based on the results of these electrochemical studies, the new PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite is an ultra-stable and high-performance supercapacitor. The improvement of the electrochemical properties of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite in comparison with its components confirmed the synergistic effect between the cobalt oxide and the PDJA. Also, two symmetric devices were designed separately for the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA. The symmetric device of PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite turned on the LED lamp for 80 s.

Jeffamine (composed of a set of polyether amines (PEA)) was provided by Huntsman company with MW about 240 g mol−1. Cobalt (׀׀) acetate tetrahydrate (Co (CH3COO)2.4H2O), pyrrolidone (NMP), and polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) were prepared from Sigma-Aldrich. Solvents, dimethylformamide (DMF), and ethanol were prepared from Merck. Potassium hydroxide (KOH), and carbon black were prepared from Merck. All of these materials were used without purification.

For the synthesis of the Jeffamine polymer dot 1 mol of jeffamine was dissolved in a 1: 1 solution of ethanol and dimethylformamide and then placed in an ultrasonic bath for 20 min. Finally, the solution was placed in Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 150 °C for 30 h. In the last step, the obtained Jeffamine polymer dot (PDJA) was purified with a dialysis bag. The concentration of PDJA was calculated to be about 13.6 mg/ml.

For the synthesis of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite, cobalt (׀׀) acetate tetrahydrate (Co (CH3COO)2.4 H2O) and Jeffamine (molar ratio of 1: 2 M) were dissolved in 15 cc ethanol and 15cc dimethylformamide (1:1). Finally, the obtained solution was dumped in a Teflon-coated stainless-steel autoclave at 150 °C for 30 h. The purple solid precipitate was washed three times with methanol and then placed in an oven at 60 °C.

The computational formula (1), was used to calculate the quantum yield of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite.
(2) Φc = Φr ( Ac /Ar)( Ic/Ir)( ηc/ηr)
where ϕ, A, η and, I are the quantum gain and the area under the emission curve, the refractive index of the solvent, the excitation wavelength, respectively. Subtitles r and c are referred to resources (Quinine sulfate) and the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. Quinine sulfate in 1 M sulfuric acid solution was used as a reference which has a quantum yield of 54 [29].

Thermal gravimetric analysis was measured under argon gas at 0–700 °C. FTIR spectroscopy was used to calculate the IR spectra. The morphology of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite was investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) analyses. The elemental analysis was performed by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) method and the elemental map method. The X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) were recorded with an X-ray Philips X′Pert instrument. Surface absorption of nanocomposite was investigated by UV-Vis spectroscopy. The photoluminescence spectra of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer. The particle size of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA was measured using dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis. The surface charge of PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and PDJA was measured from Zeta potential analysis (model of VASCO).

The electrochemical behavior of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA as electrode materials were investigated in a three-electrode system in which the Ag/AgCl electrode, and platinum electrode as the reference electrode and counter electrode (CE), respectively. In this 3-electrode system was used, KOH (2 M) aqueous solution. To prepare the working electrode, the active material, activated carbon, and PVDF adhesive (specified ratio of 8: 1: 1) were mixed in 500 μl NMP, then 40 μl of the final mixed solution was poured onto a graphite plate with a specific area (1 * 1) and was allowed 12 h to dry completely. Advanced Electrochemical workstation of PC-controlled Autolab was used for CV, GCD, and EIS electrochemical analysis. Eq. (3) was used to calculate the specific capacitance.
(3)
C =
ʃ iVdV
2 μ m Δ V

ʃivd is the area under the curve of cv where I and V are currents and potentials at CV analysis, respectively. Δv, m, and μ are the potential window, the mass of active substance, and the scan rate, respectively. To investigate the practical application of nanocomposites in energy storage systems, symmetric supercapacitors were designed for the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA. To design a symmetric supercapacitor were used, a pair of copper plates (current collector), glass microfiber paper (separator), active materials (the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA), and KOH solution (as an electrolyte).

In this work, the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite was synthesized with a facile and one-step method using the solvothermal method. During the synthesis of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite, the Jeffamine polymer was converted into the polymer dot and simultaneously composited with cobalt oxide. Scheme 1 shows the complete steps of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite synthesis.

Fig. 1a investigated the thermogravimetric analysis of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite under argon gas at 0–700 temperature. First, in the TGA spectrum with the removal of water, 4% of the weight decreased at 34–190 °C. Then, 53% of the weight was decreased after the temperature of 190 °C due to the removal of functional groups such as NH2, OH, and COOH on the surface of the PDJA. The little amount of this weight loss is related to the free chains of (residual) Jeffamine, which are precursors in the synthesis of PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. According to the results of the TGA analysis, the ratio of PDJA: Co3O4 was 1:1. Considering to the total amount of weight loss, the results obtained from TGA are consistent with EDX analysis.

The FT-IR spectra of PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and PDJA were showed in Fig. 1b. In the FT-IR spectrum of the PDJA, the broad peak observed at 3300–3500 cm−1 corresponds to the N-H and OH stretching vibrations [30]. The peak at 2881 was related to the C-H stretching vibrations and the peaks at 1669 cm−1, 1455 cm−1, and 1103 cm−1 correspond to CO, C-N, and C-O-C, respectively. Also, the pair of peaks observed at 1455 cm−1 and 1595 cm−1 corresponds to CC, which confirms the formation of polymer dot. In the FT-IR spectrum of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite, in addition to the observed peaks related to the polymer, one peak was observed at 657 cm−1, which corresponds to the Co-O bond. The presence of the Co3O4 band confirmed the formation of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite [31].

The elemental distribution and morphology of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite were investigated by FE-SEM, HR-TEM, elemental map, and EDX analyses. Fig. 2a and b show the FE-SEM image of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite in the form of intertwined spheres that intertwined structure increases the speed of ion transfer and ultimately improve the electrochemical activity of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. The formation of these interwoven spheres confirms the synthesis of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. Fig. 2b and c show elemental map analysis for cobalt, carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen with EDX analysis for the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite, respectively. According to the distribution of cobalt, oxygen, carbon, and nitrogen elements in Fig. 2b, it was confirmed that the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite was synthesized homogeneously. Also, the results of the EDX analysis confirmed the obtained results of the TGA analysis. According to diagram of particle size distribution in Fig. 2e (obtained from Fig. 2b), the size distribution of PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite plates was calculated to be about 23–31 nm.

Fig. 3a and b show the TEM image of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite in which the interwoven morphology is visible in the TEM image. Fig. 3c shows the FFT image of the nanocomposite. According to the image, the hexagonal graphene sheet can see in the FFT image. Fig. 3d shows the HRTEM image of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite with a size of 10 nm. The lattice margins at 0.32 nm, and 0.50 nm, corresponding to the planes of (002) and (001), confirmed the presence of PDJA in the nanocomposite. The lattice margin at 0.28 nm corresponds (111) plane, which is related to the Co3O4 in the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite [32]. The SAED image of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite showed in Fig. 3e, and according to this image, the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite structure is polycrystalline [33].

The XRD spectra of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA showed in Fig. 4. The graphene oxide and graphite XRD spectra were investigated for PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite accurate identification. In the XRD spectra of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA, there was a peak at 2θ of 12.7 (corresponds to the (001) plane), which the similar to the diffraction peak of graphene oxide at 2θ of 10.3, that this shift (from 10.3 to 12.7) due to the presence of carboxyl, amine, and carbonyl functional groups, and carbonyl at the surface (or edge) of polymer dot, that these functional groups increased the spacing of sp3 sheets of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite.[34]. At the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA, an amorphous peak was observed at 2θ of 17.94–37.1, corresponding to the diffraction peak of graphite at 2θ of 26.6, which confirms the synthesis of polymer dots. Diffraction peaks related to CoO and Co3O4 were observed at 2θ= 36.54, 42.44, 61.53, 73.82, 77.66 corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), and 2θ= 38.74, 44.78, 55.64, 59.30, are corresponding to the (222), (400), (422), (511), respectively. To confirm the peaks of Co3O4 in the XRD pattern of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite, the XRD pattern of Co3O4 was reported in Fig. 1S [34]. Also, from the DebyeScherrer equation to estimate the size of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. The crystallite size of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite is about 34 nm.

Fig. 5a shows the UV-Vis of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. In the absorption spectrum of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite, there is a strong absorption band at 225–250 nm, corresponding to the transition of π-π * and n-π * bands, which are characteristic of quantum dots. Also, a weak absorption was observed in the range of 250–700 nm (λAb). The photoluminescence behavior of the PDJA and the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite were investigated in Fig. 5b. As observed, PDJA and the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite were excited at 235 (λAb) wavelengths, and finally, the emission areas of PDJA and PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite were different. The PDJA emitted at a wavelength of 460 nm (λem), but the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite was an emission peak at 470 nm (λem) and, a shoulder at 670 nm (λem) due to the synergistic effect of NH2, CO2H, and OH groups in the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite [35]. The two emission peaks of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite were attributed to the phenomenon of double fluorescence, which was observed in a small number of chromophores and is a photophysical phenomenon. Double fluorescence was an unusual photophysical phenomenon observed in most polymer dots. The quantum yield of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite was calculated at about 65.45%. [36].

The particle size of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA were reported in Fig. 6a and b. According to Fig. 6a and b the particle size of the PDJA and the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite approximately to be about 22 nm and 400 nm, respectively. The zeta potential was reported for the PDJA and the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite as − 1.1 and 14.7, respectively, in Fig. 6c. The positive zeta potential of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite is due to the presence of cobalt oxide in the structure of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. According to the studies, the increase of charge (from −1 to +14) increases the stability of the suspension through the electrostatic repulsion forces, which facilitates the diffusion and storage of charge [37].

In this study, to confirm the excellent electrochemical advantages of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite, all electrochemical analyzes such as CV, GCD, and EIS were investigated in the three-electrode system and the two-electrode system (symmetric supercapacitor).

To check the performance of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and improve its performance compared to each of the constituent components, CV and GCD electrochemical analyzes were conducted for the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA as electrode material in 2 M KOH electrolyte.

Fig. 7a shows the cyclic voltammetry of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material and PDJA electrode material at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. By comparing two voltammetry, the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material compared to the PDJA electrode material has a higher current density, which increases in current density due to the synergistic effect between the PDJA electrode material and the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material. [38]. Also, the PDJA electrode material increases the charge storage in the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material by shortening the path through electron transfer. In the cyclic voltammetry of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material, two pairs of peaks (redox behavior) attribute to the faradaic reactions between Co4+, Co3+, and Co2+, which practically confirms the quasi-capacitive behavior of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material [39,40].

The cyclic voltammetry diagram of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material shows in Fig. 7b in the potential window of − 0.5–0.8 V at different scan rates. In Fig. 7b, the intensity of the peaks increases with the increased scan rate from 10 to 100 mV/s, which shows the reversibility of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material is faster in response to charging and discharging [40]. Fig. 7c shows the cyclic voltammetry of the PDJA electrode material at a scan rate of 10–100 mV/s. As can be seen in the cyclic voltammetry of the PDJA electrode material, the current density was increased with the increase of the scan rate, which is related to the capacitive current of the PDJA electrode material. The specific capacitance using cycle voltammetry was calculated for the PDJA electrode material and the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material as 288 F g−1 and 987 F g−1, respectively. Cyclic stability was a valuable factor in electrochemical evaluation. By measuring cyclic voltammetry in the range of − 0.5–0.8 V at a scan rate of 100 mV/s up to 10,000 cycles, the stability of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material and the PDJA electrode material was determined to be 98.37%, and 91.57%, respectively. To the purpose of comparing the stability of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite, the stability of similar nanocomposites in the articles were reported in Table 1. From Table 1, it is clear that the stability of PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite is higher than similar electrode material. The initial 10 CV curves and last 10 CV curves after the cycle stability of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA were provided in Fig. 2S. For comparison, cyclic voltammetry and cyclic stability of Co3O4 were reported in Fig. 3Sa and c, respectively [41].

Fig. 8b shows the current of selected cathodic and anodic peaks (Fig. 8a) at different scan rates. The linear relationship between the current density and the scan rate confirms that the charge transfer process for the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite is fast and also has good reversibility. It also confirms the process in the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite is kinetically quick diffusion controlled.

Fig. 8c and d show the GCD diagram of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material and the PDJA electrode material at the current density of 1–5 A g−1 in the potential window of 0–0.7 V [47]. By comparing these two curves it can be concluded the discharge time of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material was longer than the PDJA electrode material, which confirms the improvement of the supercapacitor behavior in the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material. Also, in both the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA electrode materials, the specific capacitance increased with decreasing current density. The difference in the appearance of the GCD diagram of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA confirmed the improvement of the supercapacitor performance of the nanocomposite. The specific capacitance of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA were reported with GCD analysis as 800 F g−1 and 285 F g−1, respectively. For comprising, GCD of Co3O4 was reported in 3Sb. [41,48].

For the electrochemical performance perfect investigation, two symmetric devices of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA were made with copper plates as the current collector and 2 M KOH solution as the electrolyte.

Fig. 9a shows the cyclic voltammetry of the symmetric PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite device and the symmetric PDJA device in the potential window of 0–1.5 V at 2 M KOH electrolyte. By comparing the CV curves in Fig. 9a, it was clear the area under the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite curve was increased compared to the PDJA due to the higher specific capacitance of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. Fig. 9b and c show the cyclic voltammetry of the symmetric devices of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA in the potential window of 0–1.5 V at different scan rate. In both voltammograms, the current density increases with increasing scanning rate, which confirms the improvement of the electrochemical performance in the symmetric devices. As can be seen in Fig. 9b, all the CV curves at different current densities have the same shape, which confirms the low internal resistance and ideal capacitor behavior [49]. The specific capacitance of the symmetric device of PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite is 90 F g−1 at 50 mV/s. To confirm the practical application of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite symmetric device the cycle stability was investigated up to 10,000 cycles at the scan rate of 100 mV/s. According to Fig. 9d, the stability of the nanocomposite device up to 10,000 cycles at a scan rate of 100 mV/s is about 93.68% [50,51].

Fig. 10 a and b show the GCD curve of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite and the PDJA devices at a current density of 1–5 A g−1. By comparing these two curves, it was clear that the discharge time of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite was higher than the PDJA, and the supercapacitor behavior of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite was improved.

Finally, impedance analysis was performed to complete the electrochemical results of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite two-electrode system. Fig. 10c shows the Nyquist diagram of the symmetric device of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. The Nyquist diagram of the symmetric device of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite was composed of two parts the vertical line at low frequency, which refers to the non-faradaic mechanism, and a small semicircle at high frequency, which refers to the charge transfer at the electrode/electrolyte surface and very partial faradaic mechanism. Fig. 10d shows an enlarged view of the semicircle in the high-frequency part of the nanocomposite. The small diameter of the semicircle refers to the favorable electrochemical kinetics of the symmetric device of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite, which increases the power density. As can be seen in the Figure, the vertical line at a low frequency shifted towards the vertical wall, which confirms the ideal capacitor behavior. To fit the impedance data an equivalent circuit was proposed based on the Nyquist diagram (inner part of Fig. 10c). The results are reported in Table 2 for the symmetric device of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. This equivalent circuit includes electrical elements RS, RCT, CPE1-T, CPE2-T, and CPE-P. The RS shows the equivalent series resistance containing electrolyte resistance (KOH), current collector resistance (copper), and membrane resistance [52]. The RCT refers to the charge transfer resistance related to the faradaic reaction on the surface of the electrodes, and CPE-T constant phase elements correspond to the non-ideal action of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. The CPE-P is a dimensionless numerical unit that checks the deviation of the capacitor behavior. In other words, the closer this parameter is to 1, is confirmed the ideal capacitor behavior. [53,54].

Finally, the symmetric the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite device turned on the LED lamp according to Scheme 2, which confirmed the practical application of the symmetric device made with the new the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite.

Quantum dots, which have attracted much attention today, when combined with metal oxides, have ideal supercapacitor properties due to the synergistic effect between the metal oxide and the quantum dot. Considering this advantage, in this study, we investigated a new class of quantum dots, including polymer dots in the supercapacitor’s application. In summary, the synthesis of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite through a facile and one-step method using Jeffamine polymer and cobalt acetate. In this method, in one step, Jeffamine was converted into a polymer dot, and simultaneously cobalt acetate formed the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite. The photoluminescence properties of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite were investigated, confirming that the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite emits in both infrared and visible regions. The electrochemical performance of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite was investigated in the two-electrode system and the three-electrode system. In the three-electrode system, the supercapacitor properties of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material and the PDJA electrode material were, compared and investigated. The results of the electrochemical analysis confirmed that the specific capacitance of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite electrode material and the PDJA electrode material was 987 F g−1 and 283 F g−1, respectively, and this increase in specific capacitance of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite compared to the PDJA is due to the synergistic effect between the PDJA and cobalt oxide. Finally, the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite symmetric supercapacitor and the PDJA symmetric supercapacitor were made using the copper current collector, and 2 M KOH electrolytes. By comparing the results of the electrochemical analyses of these two symmetrical devices, it was confirmed that the specific capacitance of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite symmetric device was five times that of the PDJA symmetric device. finally, the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite symmetric device turned on the LED lamp, which confirmed the practical application of the PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite material as a supercapacitor with high stability and optimal conductivity. From this study, it can be concluded that the new PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite is a suitable material for manufacturing supercapacitors with high stability and ideal performance. The new PDJA-Co3O4 nanocomposite is the basis for the preparation and synthesis of the nanocomposites based on polymer dots for electrochemical and supercapacitor applications.

Fariba Alimola carried out the experiment. Fariba Alimola wrote the manuscript with support from Nasser Arsalani. Nasser Arsalani supervised the project. All authors discussed the results and contributed to the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz for the financial support for this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.169851.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
